introSection: Screamin' Eagle is a wooden roller coaster located at Six Flags St. Louis in Eureka, Missouri, USA. Opened in 1976, it was the last coaster PTC built and the last one built by John C. Allen.

sections.title: History
sections.content:
Screamin' Eagle opened on April 10, 1976.

For the 1990 season, Screamin' Eagle was repainted and the rolling stock was replaced. The park claimed that due to reduced friction between the trains and track, the maximum speed would be increased to.

The first drop was retracked in 2003.

On June 21, 2016, American Coaster Enthusiasts recognized the ride as an ACE Roller Coaster Landmark.

In 2022, the ride received a new entrance plaza, as well as a new ADA access ramp.

For 2026, the lift hill was retracked.

sections.title: Trains
sections.content: Screamin' Eagle originally had three-bench trains from the Philadelphia Toboggan Company. These were replaced in 1990 with new two-bench trains, also from PTC.

sections.title: Incident
sections.content: Two trains bumped on September 18, 1976. Of the 60 riders, 19 were "shaken up", with three advised by park officials to go to a hospital for further examination.

fullText:
Screamin' Eagle is a wooden roller coaster located at Six Flags St. Louis in Eureka, Missouri, USA. Opened in 1976, it was the last coaster PTC built and the last one built by John C. Allen.

Screamin' Eagle opened on April 10, 1976.

For the 1990 season, Screamin' Eagle was repainted and the rolling stock was replaced. The park claimed that due to reduced friction between the trains and track, the maximum speed would be increased to.

The first drop was retracked in 2003.

On June 21, 2016, American Coaster Enthusiasts recognized the ride as an ACE Roller Coaster Landmark.

In 2022, the ride received a new entrance plaza, as well as a new ADA access ramp.

For 2026, the lift hill was retracked.



Red track and white supports.

Screamin' Eagle originally had three-bench trains from the Philadelphia Toboggan Company. These were replaced in 1990 with new two-bench trains, also from PTC.

Two trains bumped on September 18, 1976. Of the 60 riders, 19 were "shaken up", with three advised by park officials to go to a hospital for further examination.
